topic: Someone receives flowers with no card attached
raw_output:
[story 1]

The small, brightly colored bouquet sat on the entryway table, an unexpected splash of vibrant life against the muted tones of the hallway. Elias picked up a rose, his fingers trembling slightly as he examined the velvety petals. There was no note, no inscription, just the silent offering of blooms. He kept glancing toward the front door, half-expecting someone to materialize and demand an explanation. His breath hitched in his chest; the silence of the apartment suddenly felt heavy, amplifying the sound of his own quickening pulse. He tucked the stems awkwardly under his arm, feeling a sudden, overwhelming urge to flee the space before he could say something entirely inappropriate.

[story 2]

When Clara discovered the arrangement, she froze mid-step. The arrangement itself was exquisite—deep purple irises and creamy lilies—but the absence of any accompanying paper made her stomach clench. She turned the vase slowly in her hands, her gaze darting around the living room as if searching for a hidden message or a culprit. A faint sheen of perspiration broke out on her forehead. "Who...?" she managed, her voice coming out higher pitched than she intended. Her hands clenched into tight fists at her sides, the delicate paper of the wrapping feeling suddenly flimsy and inadequate against the weight of the unspoken question hanging in the air.

[story 3]

Mark stared at the flowers as if they were a foreign object placed suddenly in his meticulously ordered workspace. The sheer unexpectedness of the gesture caused a visible tightening around his jaw. He circled the desk twice, his movements jerky and uncoordinated. He reached out a tentative finger to touch a petal, pulling his hand back quickly, as if the flower might burn him. A knot formed low in his stomach, a churning sensation that made his throat feel dry. He couldn't seem to focus on anything else; his eyes kept flicking toward the window, hoping the outside world would offer a distraction from the sudden, intense focus on the silent gift.
